chunk_summary: Management of severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in infants under 6 months focuses on breastfeeding restoration, safe feeding alternatives, and stabilization of acute issues like hypoglycemia and infection, with close monitoring and follow-up for sustained recovery.
learning_objectives: Understand the therapeutic goals for SAM management in infants under 6 months, including breastfeeding restoration and prevention of complications.
Learn techniques such as supplementary suckling and relactation to support feeding in malnourished infants.
Recognize safety protocols for SAM management, including frequent small feeds, warmth maintenance, and infection treatment.
Identify discharge criteria and follow-up requirements for infants recovering from SAM.
key_facts: Direct breastfeeding with skilled observation is prioritized; expressed breast milk or supplementary suckling technique (SST) may be used if the infant is weak.
Starter therapeutic feeds are temporary and should avoid overly concentrated formulas to prevent increased solute load.
Non-breastfed infants require program-approved replacement feeding with strict hygiene counseling and close follow-up.
Daily reassessment includes feeding effectiveness, urine output, temperature, weight trend, and maternal barriers.
Discharge criteria include effective feeding, clinical stability, and clear weight gain on the home-feeding plan.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Management of acute malnutrition in infants under 6 months is feeding-first and dyad-focused. The therapeutic goal is restoration of effective breastfeeding (or safe alternative feeding) while preventing hypoglycemia, hypothermia, and infection. (Source: WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children)
document_key_topics: Safe feeding alternatives for non-breastfed infants
Stabilization of acute complications in SAM
Discharge and follow-up criteria for malnourished infants
